principles: Always index foreign key columns used in JOIN operations to improve join performance
Create composite indexes for common multi-column WHERE and ORDER BY patterns
Avoid creating many overlapping indexes that hurt write performance
Consider covering indexes (include non-key columns) to reduce random I/O on large tables
Prefer selective columns for indexes; low-selectivity columns (e.g., boolean flags) often don't help
Index columns used in WHERE clauses, especially those with high selectivity
Index columns used in ORDER BY and GROUP BY clauses when combined with WHERE filters
Use partial indexes for filtered queries (e.g., WHERE status = 'active')
Consider BRIN indexes for large tables with naturally ordered data (timestamps, auto-increment IDs)
Monitor index usage and remove unused indexes to improve write performance
antiPatterns: Full table scans on very large tables without any index usage
Indexes on columns that are never used in filters, joins, or sorts
Multiple indexes with the same leading column but slightly different tails without clear need
Indexing every column in a table (over-indexing)
Creating indexes on low-cardinality columns (e.g., gender, status with few values) without justification
bestPractices: Analyze query patterns before creating indexes
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify index usage
Use unique indexes to enforce data integrity
Consider index-only scans when possible (covering indexes)
